meta.purpose: Examples that show judgment structure rather than scripts. Performance diagnosis and transformation patterns.
meta.load_condition: Load when examples, transformation patterns, or before/after critique are needed.

cases.title: A card expand animation animates width instead of transform: scaleX.
cases.context: A card expand animation animates width instead of transform: scaleX.
cases.what_happened: Animating width triggers layout recalculation on every frame, affecting sibling elements, causing visible jank at 15-20fps.
cases.what_was_learned: The root cause is a structural problem that can be diagnosed and fixed with domain-specific patterns.
cases.structural_pattern: Use transform: scaleX for the visual scaling, set the final width via CSS class after the animation completes, and apply overflow: hidden to the container.

cases.title: Scroll-linked animation reads offsetTop inside a scroll handler.
cases.context: Scroll-linked animation reads offsetTop inside a scroll handler.
cases.what_happened: Reading offsetTop forces synchronous layout on every scroll event, blocking the main thread and creating visible scroll lag.
cases.what_was_learned: The root cause is a structural problem that can be diagnosed and fixed with domain-specific patterns.
cases.structural_pattern: Cache layout values on resize and scroll with requestAnimationFrame batching. Use IntersectionObserver for visibility triggers instead of manual offset calculations.

cases.title: A developer adds `will-change: transform` to 200 elements to 'optimize' a list animation.
cases.context: A developer adds `will-change: transform` to 200 elements to 'optimize' a list animation.
cases.what_happened: Each `will-change: transform` creates a new compositor layer with its own GPU texture. 200 elements × ~2MB per texture = 400MB GPU memory. On mobile devices, this triggers GPU memory pressure, texture thrashing, and ultimately a browser crash or forced layer eviction that makes performance worse than no optimization at all.
cases.what_was_learned: The root cause is a structural problem that can be diagnosed and fixed with domain-specific patterns.
cases.structural_pattern: Apply `will-change` only to elements that will animate within the next 200ms. Add it dynamically before the animation starts via JS (`element.style.willChange = 'transform'`) and remove it after the animation ends (`element.style.willChange = 'auto'`). Never set `will-change` in a static stylesheet. Treat it as a temporary GPU memory allocation, not a permanent optimization.

cases.title: A product's onboarding flow uses animated diagrams and step transitions without any reduced-motion fallback.
cases.context: A product's onboarding flow uses animated diagrams and step transitions without any reduced-motion fallback.
cases.what_happened: The entire onboarding — animated illustrations, sliding step transitions, bouncing progress dots, rotating confirmation checkmarks — runs at full motion with no `prefers-reduced-motion` media query. Users with vestibular disorders experience dizziness or nausea. Accessibility audit fails. The product is legally non-compliant in jurisdictions requiring WCAG 2.2 Level AA.
cases.what_was_learned: The root cause is a structural problem that can be diagnosed and fixed with domain-specific patterns.
cases.structural_pattern: Wrap all animations in a media query check: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { *, *::before, *::after { animation-duration: 0.01ms !important; transition-duration: 0.01ms !important; } }`. For JS-driven animations, check `window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches` and skip animations, replacing with instant state changes. Ensure that no essential information is conveyed exclusively through animation — all content must be accessible in static form.

cases.title: A hero section animation runs at 60fps on a MacBook Pro but at 22fps on a mid-range Android phone — and nobody tested the latter.
cases.context: A hero section animation runs at 60fps on a MacBook Pro but at 22fps on a mid-range Android phone — and nobody tested the latter.
cases.what_happened: Development and review happen exclusively on high-end developer machines (M1/M3 MacBooks with dedicated GPU). The animation includes 8 simultaneous elements with box-shadow transitions, gradient shifts, and blur-based parallax. These are GPU-intensive on all devices but the M-series chips absorb the cost. On a $300 Android phone, the same animation drops to 22fps with 300ms input delay. The team only discovers this after shipping, via user complaints.
cases.what_was_learned: The root cause is a structural problem that can be diagnosed and fixed with domain-specific patterns.
cases.structural_pattern: Define a device tier matrix during design: Tier 1 (flagship), Tier 2 (mid-range), Tier 3 (entry-level). Define an animation complexity budget per tier: Tier 1 allows 6 motion groups and box-shadow/blur; Tier 2 allows 3 motion groups, no blur; Tier 3 allows 2 motion groups, transform/opacity only. Test during development with Chrome DevTools CPU throttling (4-6x slowdown) to simulate Tier 3. If the animation exceeds 16.7ms frame time at 6x slowdown, reduce complexity for all tiers.
